Author: Li et al., 2021
title: Neoantigen vaccination induces clinical and immunologic responses in non-small cell lung cancer patients harboring EGFR mutations
abstract_note: BACKGROUND: Neoantigen (NeoAg) peptides displayed at the tumor cell surface by human leukocyte antigen molecules show exquisite tumor specificity and can elicit T cell mediated tumor rejection. However, few NeoAgs are predicted to be shared between patients, and none to date have demonstrated therapeutic value in the context of vaccination. METHODS: We report here a phase I trial of personalized NeoAg peptide vaccination (PPV) of 24 stage III/IV non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients who had previously progressed following multiple conventional therapies, including surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, and tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs). Primary endpoints of the trial evaluated feasibility, tolerability, and safety of the personalized vaccination approach, and secondary trial endpoints assessed tumor-specific immune reactivity and clinical responses. Of the 16 patients with epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) mutations, nine continued TKI therapy concurrent with PPV and seven patients received PPV alone. RESULTS: Out of 29 patients enrolled in the trial, 24 were immunized with personalized NeoAg peptides. Aside from transient rash, fatigue and/or fever observed in three patients, no other treatment-related adverse events were observed. Median progression-free survival and overall survival of the 24 vaccinated patients were 6.0 and 8.9 months, respectively. Within 3-4 months following initiation of PPV, seven RECIST-based objective clinical responses including one complete response were observed. Notably, all seven clinical responders had EGFR-mutated tumors, including four patients that had continued TKI therapy concurrently with PPV. Immune monitoring showed that five of the seven responding patients demonstrated vaccine-induced T cell responses against EGFR NeoAg peptides. Furthermore, two highly shared EGFR mutations (L858R and T790M) were shown to be immunogenic in four of the responding patients, all of whom demonstrated increases in peripheral blood neoantigen-specific CD8+ T cell frequencies during the course of PPV. CONCLUSIONS: These results show that personalized NeoAg vaccination is feasible and safe for advanced-stage NSCLC patients. The clinical and immune responses observed following PPV suggest that EGFR mutations constitute shared, immunogenic neoantigens with promising immunotherapeutic potential for large subsets of NSCLC patients. Furthermore, PPV with concurrent EGFR inhibitor therapy was well tolerated and may have contributed to the induction of PPV-induced T cell responses.
study_type_2: Single-arm, open-label Phase Ib clinical trial
vaccine_platform: Personalized Neoantigen Peptide Vaccine (short and long synthetic peptides) with topical Imiquimod (TLR7 agonist) adjuvan
neoantigen_source: Somatic mutations identified from targeted sequencing of 508 cancer-associated genes; focus on EGFR driver mutations and other tumor-associated mutations
peptide_length: 9â20 amino acids; mixture of short HLA-I peptides and long HLA-II peptides
combination_therapy: Some patients received PPV alone; others received PPV concurrently with EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (EGFR-TKIs)
clinical_outcome: 7/24 objective responses (1 Complete Response, 6 Partial Responses); median PFS 6.0 months; median OS 8.9 months. Responses observed exclusively in EGFR-mutated patients
success_factors: EGFR-mutated tumors; vaccine-induced neoantigen-specific CD8+ T-cell responses; targeting shared EGFR neoantigens (L858R, T790M); concurrent EGFR inhibitor therapy may enhance immune responses.
failure_limitation_factors: Small sample size; non-randomized single-arm design; lack of control group; responses mainly limited to EGFR-mutated patients; some vaccinated patients developed immune responses without clinical benefit.

Author: Ingels et al., 2024
title: Neoantigen-targeted dendritic cell vaccination in lung cancer patients induces long-lived T cells exhibiting the full differentiation spectrum
abstract_note: Summary Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is known for high relapse rates despite resection in early stages. Here, we present the results of a phase I clinical trial in which a dendritic cell (DC) vaccine targeting patient-individual neoantigens is evaluated in patients with resected NSCLC. Vaccine manufacturing is feasible in six of 10 enrolled patients. Toxicity is limited to grade 1â2 adverse events. Systemic T cell responses are observed in five out of six vaccinated patients, with T cell responses remaining detectable up to 19 months post vaccination. Single-cell analysis indicates that the responsive T cell population is polyclonal and exhibits the near-entire spectrum of T cell differentiation states, including a naive-like state, but excluding exhausted cell states. Three of six vaccinated patients experience disease recurrence during the follow-up period of 2 years. Collectively, these data support the feasibility, safety, and immunogenicity of this treatment in resected NSCLC.
study_completion_date: Follow-up to 2 years after last vaccine dose (data collected through 2023)
vaccine_platform: Autologous monocyte-derived dendritic cells (moDCs) electroporated with neoantigen-encoding mRNA (Neo-mRNA)
peptide_length: 25-mer tandem minigene + 12 residues on each side
clinical_outcome: 3/6 Disease free after 2 years, T cell responses seen up to 19 months
success_factors: Safe, immunogenic(patients responded), Responses stll detected after 19 months (induced stem cell memory),
failure_limitation_factors: Long manufacturing time (about 194 days), small cohort (6 vaccinated), 40% manufacturing failure rate, 1 patient with STK11 mutation showed no T cell response

Author: Awad et al., 2022
title: Personalized neoantigen vaccine NEO-PV-01 with chemotherapy and anti-PD-1 as first-line treatment for non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer
abstract_note: Summary Neoantigens arising from mutations in tumor DNA provide targets for immune-based therapy. Here, we report the clinical and immune data from a Phase Ib clinical trial of a personalized neoantigen-vaccine NEO-PV-01 in combination with pemetrexed, carboplatin, and pembrolizumab as first-line therapy for advanced non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). This analysis of 38 patients treated with the regimen demonstrated no treatment-related serious adverse events. Multiple parameters including baseline tumor immune infiltration and on-treatment circulating tumor DNA levels were highly correlated with clinical response. De novo neoantigen-specific CD4+ and CD8+ T cell responses were observed post-vaccination. Epitope spread to non-vaccinating neoantigens, including responses to KRAS G12C and G12V mutations, were detected post-vaccination. Neoantigen-specific CD4+ T cells generated post-vaccination revealed effector and cytotoxic phenotypes with increased CD4+ T cell infiltration in the post-vaccine tumor biopsy. Collectively, these data support the safety and immunogenicity of this regimen in advanced non-squamous NSCLC.
location: Dana Farber Cancer Institute (Boston), Sarah Cannon Research Institute (Nashville), UCLA (Los Angeles), Washington University (St. Louis)
vaccine_platform: Synthetic peptides mixed with poly-ICLC adjuvant, administered under skin
combination_therapy: Pemetrexed + carboplatin + pembrolizumab (continued up to 2 years)
success_factors: Safe and well-tolerated, robust neoantigen-specific CD4+ and CD8+ T cell induction, epitope spreading to driver mutations (KRAS), strong humoral responses.
failure_limitation_factors: Single-arm design -cant attribute clinical benefit to vaccine vs. chemo + pembrolizumab alone, higher female ratio, lower PD-L1 expression in VAX cohort.

Author: Zhuo et al., 2024
title: A phase I study of a common neoantigen srRNA vaccine (JCXH-212) with or without toripalimab in patients with non- small cell lung cancer(NSCLC)
abstract_note: srRNA-based universal neoantigen vaccine (alone or + toripalimab) was safe and induced neoantigen-specific T-cell responses in pre-treated advanced NSCLC.
clinical_outcome: 1 patient with lesion decrease, 1 with Stable Disease for â¥12 weeks, induced neoantigen-specific IFN T-cell responses
success_factors: Modest safety, induction of antigen-specific T-cell immunity

Author: McCann et al., 2022
title: Targeting the tumor mutanome for personalized vaccination in a TMB low non-small cell lung cancer
abstract_note: Personalized neoantigen peptide vaccination mixed with poly-ICLC was feasible, safe, and immunogenic in patients with TMB-low NSCLC.
clinical_outcome: Vaccine induced de novo T-cell responses in 6 out of 7 vaccinated patients.
success_factors: Feasible and immunogenic in low TMB tumors.

Author: Ott et al., 2020
title: A Phase Ib Trial of Personalized Neoantigen Therapy Plus Anti-PD-1 in Patients with Advanced Melanoma, Non-small Cell Lung Cancer, or Bladder Cancer
abstract_note: Personalized neoantigen peptide vaccine (NEO-PV-01) combined with nivolumab induced broad de novo neoantigen-specific T-cell responses in advanced melanoma, NSCLC and bladder cancer.
population_studied: Adults with advanced melanoma, NSCLC or urothelial carcinoma
vaccine_platform: Personalized synthetic long peptide vaccine (NEO-PV-01) + poly-ICLC adjuvant
neoantigen_source: Patient-specific somatic non-synonymous mutations identified by tumor-normal WES and RNA-seq
clinical_outcome: Safe and feasible; induced durable de novo CD4? and CD8? neoantigen-specific T-cell responses with evidence of epitope spreading and encouraging clinical activity
success_factors: Personalized WES/RNA-seq pipeline, RNA-expression filtering, NetMHCpan-based prioritization, high-quality peptide manufacturing, concurrent PD-1 blockade
failure_limitation_factors: Small Phase I study, non-randomized, heterogeneous tumour types, no control arm, not powered for efficacy, only patients with sufficient mutations could receive vaccine
